attributes.stem:
A 77-year-old woman is reviewed on the surgical ward with acute confusion and agitation one day following repair of an incarcerated inguinal hernia. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She was well and oriented when she went to sleep the previous night.

On examination, she appears agitated. Her pulse rate is 85/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. Her surgical wound appears clean with no signs of infection. She is demanding to leave the hospital and has become verbally abusive. Laboratory investigations and urinalysis are within normal limits. Non-pharmacological measures including reorientation, one-on-one nursing care, and transfer to a well-lit room have been unsuccessful.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate pharmacological management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with hyperactive delirium, which is common in elderly post-operative patients with an incidence of 15-25%. The acute onset, fluctuating course, and altered consciousness with agitation are characteristic features. After excluding organic causes and implementing non-pharmacological measures, pharmacological management may be necessary. In most cases of delirium an oral medication should be offered and tried before resorting to physical restraint and parenteral administration.

Risperidone 0.5 mg orally is the most appropriate choice as it is a low-dose antipsychotic that can effectively manage symptoms of hyperactive delirium. It has a good safety profile and is preferred as first-line pharmacological therapy when non-pharmacological measures have failed.

Olanzapine intramuscular administration is too aggressive as a first-line approach and requires involuntary treatment orders in most jurisdictions. While olanzapine can be used for delirium, oral administration of low-dose antipsychotics is preferred initially.

Midazolam and temazepam, both benzodiazepines, should be avoided in elderly patients with delirium as they can worsen confusion and increase the risk of falls. They are only indicated if alcohol withdrawal is suspected, which is unlikely given the timing of symptom onset.

Morphine is not indicated as there is no evidence of pain, and opioids can worsen delirium in elderly surgical patients. The patient's post-operative pain is reportedly well-controlled.

The management of delirium should focus on identifying and treating underlying causes, implementing non-pharmacological measures, and using low-dose antipsychotics when necessary. Regular monitoring and reassessment are essential to ensure response to treatment and to detect any complications.
learningPoints: Oral risperidone at low doses is the preferred first-line pharmacological treatment for hyperactive delirium after non-pharmacological measures have failed.
Delirium is common in elderly post-operative patients with an incidence of 15-25%.
Benzodiazepines and opioids should be avoided in elderly patients with delirium as they may worsen confusion.
references: Marcantonio E. Delirium in Hospitalized Older Adults. NEJM. 2017; 377:1456-1466. Available at: https://www.nejm.org/doi/full/10.1056/NEJMcp1605501
